name: Bosnian Pyramid Energy
description: The 'Bosnian Pyramid Energy' concept is a core tenet of the controversial theory that hills near Visoko, Bosnia and Herzegovina, are actually ancient, man-made pyramids. Proponents, primarily led by Semir Osmanagić, claim these 'pyramids' (especially the 'Pyramid of the Sun') emit measurable energy fields, including electromagnetic radiation, ultrasound, and negative ions, which they believe possess healing and other beneficial properties. They propose that these energy emissions are evidence of advanced ancient technology and an interconnected global network of pyramids designed for human well-being. Osmanagić asserts the 'pyramids' are significantly older than known civilizations, dating back tens of thousands of years. Mainstream archaeology and geology overwhelmingly reject the pyramid claim, citing the hills as natural geological formations. The purported energy phenomena are attributed to natural geological processes and atmospheric conditions, rather than artificial structures or advanced technology. Scientific studies conducted by independent researchers have failed to replicate the anomalous energy readings claimed by Osmanagić and his team. The theory has faced criticism for lacking rigorous scientific methodology, relying on anecdotal evidence and misinterpretations of geological data. The 'energy' claims are often linked to broader New Age beliefs about subtle energies and ancient wisdom.
subtitle: Energy fields of the Bosnian 'pyramids'.

claims.claim: The Bosnian 'Pyramids' emit focused beams of energy.
claims.evidence: Detection of electromagnetic radiation, ultrasound, and negative ions at the 'Pyramid of the Sun'.
claims.criticism: Mainstream scientists attribute these readings to natural geological formations and atmospheric phenomena, not artificial structures. Independent studies have not replicated the claimed energy beams.

claims.claim: These energy fields have healing and beneficial properties.
claims.evidence: Anecdotal reports of improved health and well-being from visitors to the site.
claims.criticism: Such reports are anecdotal and lack scientific validation. The placebo effect and confirmation bias are likely factors.

claims.claim: The 'Pyramids' are part of a global energy network.
claims.evidence: Similar energy signatures are found at other alleged pyramid sites around the world.
claims.criticism: The existence of these 'energy signatures' at other sites is also disputed, and the claim of a global network lacks credible evidence. The term 'pyramid' is often loosely applied to any geometrically shaped hill or mountain.

keyFigures.name: Semir Osmanagić
keyFigures.role: Lead proponent and researcher of the Bosnian Pyramid theory.

keyFigures.name: Enver Imamović
keyFigures.role: Archaeologist and critic of the Bosnian Pyramid theory.

mythologyConnections: Possible connections to local Balkan folklore regarding ancient giants or deities associated with mountains.
Speculative links to ancient sun worship traditions.

timeline.event: Semir Osmanagić announces his theory about the Bosnian Pyramids.
Ongoing excavations and promotion of the theory, alongside continued scientific criticism.

sources.author: Semir Osmanagić

sources.title: Semir Osmanagić
